title: Pasta fails to start when IPv6 is completely disabled (no kernel module loaded) - "L4 socket: Address family not supported by protocol"
body:
### Issue Description

When IPv6 is fully disabled at the kernel level (i.e. the ipv6 module is not loaded, so /proc/sys/net/ipv6/ does not exist), Pasta fails to start rootless containers with the following error:

```
pasta failed with exit code 1:
L4 socket: Address family not supported by protocol
Failed to bind port 9201 (Address family not supported by protocol) for option '-t 9201-9201:9200-9200'
```


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce:
1. Disable IPv6 completely:
* Do not load the ipv6 kernel module (common on servers for reduced attack surface).
* Confirm with: sysctl net.ipv6.conf.all.disable_ipv6 -> returns "No such file or directory"
2. Attempt to run any container that uses port publishing:
```
podman run -p 9201:9200 opensearch:1.3.20
```
or via `podman compose`

### Describe the results you received

Pasta aborts with exit code 1. Users must manually specify `--network pasta:-4` (or `network_mode: "pasta:-4"` in compose) as a workaround.

### Describe the results you expected

Pasta should gracefully fall back to IPv4-only mode when it detects that IPv6 is unavailable at the kernel level, or at minimum, not treat the failure to create an IPv6 socket as fatal.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

ipv6 kernel module is not loaded.

### Additional information

comments.body:
David, thanks for reporting this.

It's actually the same issue as https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=188, which was similar in turn to https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2424192 (fixed some months ago), and which has now a complete fix, but I forgot to close it.

The latest upstream release of passt [2026_05_26.038c51e](https://archives.passt.top/passt-user/20260527093624.6349111e@elisabeth/) should have all the changes you need, and I just checked things there. Note that we only support current upstream versions here. Please close this if that works for you.

@sbrivio-rh If I believe your recent update in https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=188 and the updates I indirectly got from you via the Red Hat support, this problem is actually not fully fixed for now. Waiting for your upstream (and RHEL downstream) fix ;)

(the intent here is to inform other users like us having this issue, and wrongly thinking it's currentyl fixed, while it isn't yet, but soon will be).

> [@sbrivio-rh](https://github.com/sbrivio-rh) If I believe your recent update in https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=188 and the updates I indirectly got from you via the Red Hat support, this problem is actually not fully fixed for now.

Hello there! Nice catch, indeed, https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=188 is not fully fixed.

On the other hand, as far as Podman and pasta(1) are concerned, this actually happens to be fixed in the latest upstream version, just not for passt(1) and its users. See https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20260608202448.3523957-1-sbrivio@redhat.com/ for the full details (I wasn't quite sure whether to spam you all with it... so I didn't).

> (the intent here is to inform other users like us having this issue, and wrongly thinking it's currentyl fixed, while it isn't yet, but soon will be)

That's very much appreciated, thanks. At the same time I'm fairly sure you can't hit this with the latest upstream version of pasta(1), despite the fact that the fix for https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=188 is currently incomplete.

Ok so to be more precise: if you just migrated to RHEL 9.8, and have this issue, at the moment, it's not fixed, but there is a Red Hat case opened to somehow make it work, and I guess normally it will rather soon be fixed one way or another in the RHEL 9.8 branch (tracked here for CentOS: https://gitlab.com/redhat/centos-stream/rpms/passt/-/network/c9s?ref_type=heads and once CentOS is fixed, I guess a RHEL fixed will follow soon after ). I guess RHEL 10.2 might suffer from a similar issue.
